author: @BrasilPopular
text: Do an array of horizontally placed solar panels in the roof, and underneath the solar panels you put the maximum number of VAWTs you can fit in that area. The solar panels will generate energy from the sun and protect the VAWTs from rain (and can also collect rain). And the VAWTs will generate extra energy using an otherwise unused space. (Maybe use some kind of screen or mesh to prevent birds from hitting the blades)

author: @leostarling5746
text: Maybe you have put your blades on the wrong way round. The inside of the bamboo should be facing the wind, with the trailing edge being slightly downwind. Then the vectors will work for you and it will go a lot faster. Also, it would help to shave the trailing edge  to make it thinner but that it not so important.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I suspect that is not actually possible. If they were the 'wrong way round' it would still turn - just with the wind from behind it. the leading edge on the left or right means it spins clockwise or anticlockwise. I am not sure there is a back to front here

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering you are right about the clockwise/unticlockwise spin, of course. However, in either case, the point is to have the bulge  (longer curve) in the back, so that its under pressure will pull roughly in the direction of the spin, even when the relative wind shifts to be more of a head wind. What you have got is a plane propeller with the bulge in the front but that converts energy into wind, whereas you are trying to do the opposite, so you need the bulge at the back.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @leostarling5746 mate - I copied this design - it's not mine I just made it in bamboo - maybe you should take it up with them - either way - I still think it doesn't really matter as from the way I see it there is no 'back' all that will happen is the wind will run it from behind rather than in front

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering Sorry, I am clearly not explaining myself well enough. By front and back I meant windward and leeward.  This is the sort of thing that needs a quick vector wind/forces diagram. Just because you copied the design does not mean it is the best one, even if it might work to some extent. There is all sorts of rubbish on the internet :) As you yourself rightly said, if you stick almost anything in the wind, it will move :) It just seems a shame to miss the full benefit after all that work.

replies.author: @knot4real
replies.text:
Ive made a few wind turbines ,treadmill motors with pvc pipes , yes it will still turn with the blades installed how you have them but you are losing a LOT of energy like that , you have them installed like the motor is going to drive it like a plane!
Oh and I would suggest balancing them by weight as it will shake itself apart at useable rpm. 
Have a quick look on eBay for wind generator,a picture tells a thousand words ,

replies.author: @bugz000
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering thanks for getting back to me, local timber dealer here you can get em near tenner each or closer to a fiver with VAT discount + bulk if you dive into all that stuff - but i will say £18 for that size is the cheapest i've ever seen... i didn't believe it i had to go check, it's £18.92 pre-vat,  so closer to £22.70 post-vat, then add postage ontop, i can't see any mention of free postage, but that's fine it is big... they've only got one left in stock though...  it's very promising they've come down so far, go back 10 years a post like that would set you back £80, it doesn't grow anywhere near that big in the UK so the price ramped up dramatically

replies.author: @matthewellisor5835
replies.text:
As I understand it (and from much abused memory,) at ideal windspeed for a particular airfoil, 3 bladed will give a maximum of ~80% of theoretically available energy (remember the idea from a few videos back of 'how much work is done on the building wall? ' because if the wall doesn't move, no work was done?)

Again, as is my understanding:
Case of 1) You can't effectively balance without a counterweight that matches the expected wind speed. And it just look weird, shouldn't that count for something?
Case of 2) Gives yaw forces when it is turning to face the wind (Think household-scale turbine with a tail to face whatever wind they might see.) and it sees a "wind shadow" when each blade crosses the tower.
Case of 3) Gets an easy balance and, while it still sees loads from shadowing, it's 1/√2 (analogue to three-phase AC) and more averages out.
Case for =>4) you're only gaining 2-5%, only at your highest wind speed, while drastically increasing your construction and maintenance costs.

Whoever here knows more than this ::pointing at self:: adventurer, I'd love to learn the cases where I'm missing something! 

Even if (and that's a bold "if") my limited comprehension shares the same street with Correct, there is likely some situation where it breaks down.

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
I've heard from so many scholars or experts in renewable energy that the output from a homebrew turbine or on a given site just isn't worth it. While I only barely disagree, I've started answering that they should multiply a couple of Watts by the number of seconds they've been in their field. Starts to work out to a lot of Joules.

~20 years ago I built a water wheel in a tiny steam and fit it with an old appliance motor (I can't even remember exactly what it was from) and left it at a tiny cabin (~128sq.ft) that was used by various family and friends who needed a place to "get away" charging a pair of old batteries that couldn't start the tractor anymore.
I got only 12-25W but it kept lights, fans and radios going. 

As far as I know and I would expect it's still running, didn't cost anything, saved all those parts going to scrap or landfill and a conservative estimate is 3 MWh produced.

A long story to say that a little bit now, at little cost, comes out to a lot and is a heap more than multiplication of zero because I couldn't afford a "proper turbine" at the time.

That's one of the reasons I enjoy this channel so very much. Thank you!

author: @pulesjet
text: How did you attach the blades to the hub and not split the bolt holes ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a myth that bamboo will split on bolting

replies.author: @pulesjet
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering No I've had it happen to me.  Using washers prevents it to the most parts.  Tighten up your bolts to any reasonable compression and yours will split too.

replies.author: @pulesjet
replies.text: Using  Cupped spring washers (used cup to material )  forcing the joint tight seems to be the ticket.

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Hi Rob,
Why do you taper the blades? (I know it changes the chord angle, but it loses area) I also understand why 'propeller' blades taper, but for windmilling it seems that a larger surface towards the tip extracts more energy from the air-stream (and not just because leverage etc).
I cut non-taper blades from pipes by skewing the cut to maintain the correct angle of attack for the chord. They do put out more torque than tapered blades. But I haven't done any in-depth stuff with any; yet. ;-)
The skew is a bit tricky as its related to the ratio of axis-root and axis-tip radius.
About 70-75% towards the tip for widest cord is probably optimal, but for percentages that's a bit nerdy.
Thanks for sharing.

Maybe pure wooden blades can be reused, shreded as woodchips and biodegrade into fertilizer but we cover them with fiberglass for more strength.
